title: Cyprus Workshop Report: The Secondary Publication Right
content:
Blog post by Giorgos Glossiotis

On Monday, 16 March 2026, KR21 organised a workshop, with the support of the Cyprus Association of Librarians – Information Scientists (CALIS, ΚΕΒΕΠ), titled “The Secondary Publication Right: Concept, Legal Framework, and the Bulgarian Experience”, which was held in Nicosia, at the University of Cyprus Library. The event was led by Ana Lazarova, KR21 national coordinator for Bulgaria.

Following a warm welcome from CALIS President Theodora Petrou, participants were introduced to KR21’s new campaign, “Let Libraries be Libraries,”
creator: KR21 Guest

title: 14 April Webinar | Closed Licences, Closed Doors: Open Licensing, AI, and the Future of Research Impact
content:
When?

  • Date: 14 April 2026
  • Time: 11:00 – 12:30 CEST / 10:00 – 11:30 BST

What?

Open licensing and copyright are important aspects of publishing research, but what do they mean in practice? Creative Commons (CC) licences offer a standardised framework for sharing research outputs, with open access being increasingly mandated by international funders. However, growing concern about research outputs being used to train generative AI tools has led some authors to seek more restrictive licences. Yet the question remains: Does a more restrictive licence stop AI

title: Portuguese Civil Society Proposes Secondary Publication Rights and Obligation
content:
Blog post by Eduardo Leonardo dos Santos

On 5 February, the magnificent Great Hall of the prestigious Academy of Sciences of Lisbon hosted Portugal’s first conference in Portugal on Secondary Publication Rights (SPR). Not even an extraordinary storm that struck Lisbon for several days could prevent an afternoon of lively discussion on the role legislation should play in advancing Open Access to publicly funded scientific outputs.

The event was organised by civil society organisations Knowledge Rights 21, COMMUNIA, and the Portuguese Association of Librarians, Archivists and Information and

title: Two Europes in Five Minutes
content:
Within a few minutes of each other yesterday in Strasbourg, Members of the European Parliament voted through two motions relevant to how we regulate knowledge creation, sharing and application to support research and innovation. Neither has any immediate legal impact, but still set a direction that may well shape future, legislative, measures, for better or for worse. 

And that direction is… confused. 

The first to be voted on was a report on Generative AI and Copyright, prepared on the initiative of the Parliament’s Legal Affairs Committee, and led by Axel Voss MEP. The second was a motion for resolution on the upcoming European Research Area Act, led by Eszter Lakos MEP.  

This blog explores what each says about how the EU should regulate knowledge, and where the risk of confusion arises. 

The Texts

Axel

title: The UK’s Copyright Laws Will Hobble Its AI Ambitions
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published on 11 December 2025 on the Times Higher Education

Artificial intelligence played a crucial role in enabling the safe and rapid development and roll-out of the Covid vaccine. Using scientific journal articles, published clinical trials, databases and other resources, scientists were able to use machine learning models to quickly prioritise fruitful research pathways, allowing them to bypass traditional bottlenecks associated with vaccine development and enhance the precision of antigen selection.

In the US, where Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna developed the mRNA vaccine, the relevant data laws support such use of information by commercial entities and universities alike. But under UK copyright law, AstraZeneca scientists – who co-developed the Oxford-AstraZeneca viral vector vaccine – would only have been allowed to “mine” the tens if not hundreds of thousands of potentially r

title: Safeguard Access, Empower Europe: Launching KR21’s Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries
content:
The mission of libraries is under threat. Not just from budget cuts or censorship, but from a “silent” crisis: a legal and market framework that is systematically stripping libraries of their right to function. Libraries are being written out of the digital age. It’s time to write them back in.

Today, Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) launches a definitive roadmap to fight back: Safeguard Access, Empower Europe – An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries. This is a sister publication to our Action Plan on Knowledge for a Stronger Europe, focused on research and open science.

Download the Action Plan

title: The Political Science of Open Science
content:
This article summarises the results of a round-table discussion at the Science|Business annual conference held in Brussels on 4-5 February 2026. We are grateful for the engagement of other participants whose ideas have shaped what is presented here.

The European Research Area (ERA) Act, now due to be published in the 4th quarter of 2026, has been promoted as the main legislative game in town when it comes to making a reality of the freedom of circulation of knowledge throughout the EU. Advancing open access and open science are central to this goal.

While the ERA Act is historic as the first general research-focused piece of legislation tabled by the European Commission, there has been plenty of other work already in this space. Through funding and the conditions applied to funding, the EU has set and shaped standards and practices around open access and open science for many years. It has also provided the space and structure for discussions among different stakeholders, enabling the debate to move forward.

This raises the question of where and how the ERA Act can add value beyond the work already taking place. This is a cha
